observatory-work-1.text: The observatory is open to everyone. Anyone can use its capabilities to obtain images of space objects. Moreover, the site has a section

about.title: About the Project
about.description: Learn more about the Look at the Stars project, our team, and the DIY astronomical observatory. Join us and discover the beauty of the cosmos!
about.intro: , and this site is my attempt to share my hobby with you, to bring together all the information and capture the achievements made on the path to exploring the cosmos. Astronomy for me is not just a hobby, but an entire world that has changed my life. Through it I have found incredible friends, learned much that is new, and realized how vast and mysterious the expanses of the Universe are. This project is our shared contribution to the popularization and development of astronomy. We sincerely believe that through our efforts the cosmos will become closer and more accessible to everyone.
about.telegram-channel: Subscribe to the author's Telegram channel for this project
about.team-description: We work in very different fields, but we are all united by one thing — a love of stars and the night sky. Since 2016 we have been popularizing astronomy, organizing star parties in the Orenburg region, and holding sidewalk astronomy evenings in Orenburg. Together we look at the stars, share knowledge, and inspire others.
about.project-description: In 2016 in the Orenburg region we launched the Look at the Stars project to make astronomy accessible to everyone. Since then, every season we hold free lectures under the open sky, organize trips outside the city with telescopes, and run informal sidewalk astronomy evenings.
about.project-goal: The main goal of the project is to show the greatness and beauty of the Universe, to inspire people to study the cosmos. We cover not only the Orenburg region but also neighboring regions, traveling to places where the night sky is especially bright. You can learn more about our current and past events on the page
about.project-sidewalk: , and for those who want to see the stars within the city we have prepared the section
about.project-sidewalk-link: Where to Look Through a Telescope in Orenburg
about.project-locations: Our events take place not only in the cities and towns of the Orenburg region, but also in neighboring regions. We organize field trips to remote locations where minimal light pollution allows you to enjoy the starry sky to the fullest. Our team of amateur astronomers and volunteers shares their knowledge and experience so that everyone can discover the beauty and mysteries of the Universe.
about.project-lectures: One of the most important components of the project are lectures delivered by experienced amateur astronomers. We talk about scientific discoveries, the history of astronomy, and its modern achievements. Lectures are held in a light format where complex things are explained in simple and clear language. This helps listeners not only broaden their horizons but also inspires independent study of astronomy.
about.project-sidewalk-astronomy: We pay special attention to sidewalk astronomy evenings. These open-air gatherings take place in the most accessible locations in the city. At such events anyone can come up, look through a telescope, and learn more about what the starry sky holds.
about.observatory-intro: This project is one of the most significant stages of my life. Our DIY astronomical observatory is a unique phenomenon for the Orenburg region. It operates autonomously and allows obtaining images of deep-space objects, making astronomy accessible to all. More details about the operation and equipment of the observatory can be found in the section —
about.observatory-work-2: , where you will find an archive of FITS files. These data can be used for training, asteroid searching, creating astrophotographs, and other scientific or creative purposes.
about.observatory-photos-1: Soon we will launch a free telescope control service so that even more people can appreciate the beauty of the night sky. Don't forget to visit the section
about.observatory-photos-2: , which contains images obtained with the help of the observatory and at our star parties. These photographs have been processed by me and my friends and are a reflection of our passion for the cosmos.
about.observatory-conclusion: This project is our contribution to the popularization of astronomy. We sincerely hope it will inspire you to discover the Universe!
about.your-help-intro: The development of the Look at the Stars project has been made possible in large part by the support of the Rodnye Goroda social investment program of Gazprom Neft and the assistance of the Orenburg Media news agency team.
about.your-help-description: Our DIY observatory project has also resonated with many engaged participants. We have ambitious plans for developing this direction, and your support plays a key role. We are grateful to everyone who helps with advice, consultations, and recommendations. If we accidentally missed anyone on the acknowledgements list, please write to us — it is very important.
about.your-help-conclusion: Thanks to your help, the cosmos truly comes closer!

index.title: Look at the Stars Project
index.description: Look at the Stars — a unique project in the Orenburg region: telescope observations outside the city, sidewalk astronomy, an observatory in the Orenburg region, and astrophotography

mailing.title: Mailings
mailing.save-first-hint: Save draft first to upload an image
mailing.launch-confirm: Are you sure? Emails will be sent to all subscribers.

objects.title: Astronomical Objects Catalog
objects.description: Catalog of astronomical objects photographed by the observatory: galaxies, nebulae, asteroids, and comets. The objects table includes information on total exposure, availability of processed photos, and data for each filter: Luminance, Red, Green, Blue, Ha, OIII, SII. Learn more about each object and the observation details conducted by the observatory.

observatory.title: Observatory
observatory.description: A DIY amateur astronomical observatory with remote access from anywhere in the world via the internet. Observatory operation statistics, number of captured frames, and accumulated data. Telescope operation calendar.
observatory.text: Observation of the Universe starts here — in real time you can see current weather conditions, camera feeds, equipment status, and the shooting schedule. The observatory operates in semi-automatic mode, allowing remote deep-sky imaging. An intelligent control system analyzes weather conditions and automatically decides when to begin operations. More details about the equipment and operating principles can be found on a dedicated page:

mount.title: Mount: Sky-Watcher EQ6
mount.description: A reliable and precise mount that keeps the telescope stable even during long exposures.

telescope.title: Telescope: Sky-Watcher BK2001P
telescope.description: A reflector telescope with a 200 mm aperture, ideal for deep-sky observations.

main-camera.title: Main Camera: ASI ZWO 6200MM Pro
main-camera.description: A highly sensitive 24 MP astronomical camera capable of capturing the finest details of distant objects.

guide-camera.title: Guide Camera: QHY QHY5
guide-camera.description: A camera for precise targeting and object tracking.

guide-scope.title: Guide Scope: SV106 Guide Scope 50mm (Helical Focuser)
guide-scope.description: A compact telescope for guiding during long-exposure imaging.

focuser.title: Focuser: ZWO EAF
focuser.description: An electronic focuser providing precise focus adjustment.

filter-wheel.title: Filter Wheel: ZWO EFW 8x31mm
filter-wheel.description: A versatile filter wheel for working with various spectral ranges.

filters.title: Filters: ZWO L, R, G, B, Ha, OIII, SII (1.25")
filters.description: A set of filters for capturing color images and narrowband imaging.

coma-corrector.title: Coma Corrector: Baader 2" Mark III MPCC
coma-corrector.description: Eliminates optical distortions, ensuring image sharpness.

observatory.description-1: The main focus of the observatory is photographing deep-sky objects such as nebulae, galaxies, and star clusters. The equipment and technologies used in the observatory allow obtaining high-quality images that can be used for both scientific research and educational purposes.
observatory.description-2: The observatory operates in semi-automatic mode, which allows it to be controlled remotely from anywhere in the world via the internet. This makes it accessible to amateur astronomers, researchers, and enthusiasts from around the world.
observatory.description-3: The observatory is open for collaboration with amateur astronomers, researchers, and educational institutions. We are happy to provide access to our data for scientific and educational purposes. If you want to become part of our community or use our resources for your projects, contact us via the feedback form.
observatory.description-4: The Orenburg Observatory is a unique combination of modern technology and the enthusiasm of amateur astronomers. We strive to make astronomy accessible to everyone interested in the mysteries of the Universe. Join us to explore the boundless expanses of space together!
observatory.equipment-description: The tools and technologies used ensure high observation accuracy and professional image quality. Here is the main equipment used in operation:
observatory.photos-and-data: A dedicated section of the site presents <photosLink>photos</photosLink> of captured <objectsLink>deep-sky objects</objectsLink>. Detailed parameters and characteristics are available for each object, such as distance, size, spectral data, and much more. These materials can be useful for both beginner astronomers and professionals.

observatory-weather.title: Orenburg Observatory Weather
observatory-weather.description: Up-to-date weather charts from the weather station of an amateur observatory near Orenburg. Temperature, humidity, cloud cover, and wind speed in real time.

photo.description: {{photoTitle}} — a photograph of an astronomical object taken at a DIY observatory, equipment used: {{equipment}}.

photos.title: Astrophoto
photos.description: A collection of astrophotographs of galaxies, star clusters, nebulae, planets, and other space objects captured by an amateur observatory. Find and filter images by categories and parameters to see unique shots of the Universe with details for each filter and exposure.

stargazing.title: Star Parties
stargazing.where_link: Where to Look Through a Telescope in Orenburg
stargazing.description: Star parties in Orenburg — exciting trips outside the city where telescopes open cosmic horizons. Evenings of scientific discovery, lectures, and lively discussion with like-minded people. Learn the rules and prepare for stargazing.
stargazing.telegram-subscription: Subscribe to the Telegram channel to stay up to date
stargazing.text-part-1: A star party in Orenburg is a chance to touch the mysteries of the Universe while observing the starry sky in complete darkness. We invite you on exciting trips outside the city where telescopes open cosmic horizons right in the fields of the Orenburg region.
stargazing.text-part-2: Each star party is an evening of scientific discoveries, inspiring lectures, and lively interaction with like-minded people. You will learn how our events work, get acquainted with the basic rules of behavior, and be able to prepare in advance for stargazing.

checkin.title: Participant Check-In
checkin.duplicate-qr: This QR code has already been scanned!

entry.title: Event Ticket
entry.show-qr: Show this QR code at the entrance

stargazing-faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
stargazing-faq.description: Find answers to common questions about star parties: registration, what to bring, cost, duration, and how to get there. Prepare for a night under the stars in comfort!

where.question: ❓How do I find out where it takes place?
where.answer: Our star parties always take place outside the city, usually within 40–70 km. We travel away from light pollution and city lights to see the stars. The exact location is available to you after registration. If you lose the location — return to the registration page via the same link. It has the geolocation — Yandex Maps and Google Maps.

registration.question: ❓Can I come without registering?
registration.answer: No, you cannot. Registration is mandatory as it allows us to plan the event, notify participants of possible changes, and ensure comfort for everyone.

qr.question: ❓What is the QR code for?
qr.answer: The QR code is your entry ticket to our event. It will be checked at the entrance. Please prepare it in advance to avoid congestion on site. If you don't have a QR code, we will be unable to admit you. The QR code is our way to regulate the number of participants at the site. Our natural landscape and the team's physical capacity have limits. Please respect this and understand.

what-to-bring.question: ❓What should I bring?
what-to-bring.answer: For a comfortable stay at the star party site, you must have warm clothes (we are in the steppe, it is windy and cold at night), a tourist mat, folding chairs, and blankets. We also recommend thermoses with tea/coffee and sandwiches. We advise bringing insect repellent. Hookahs, barbecues, alcohol, and cigarettes are prohibited! We also have the cleanest site — after a star party we don't find a single piece of litter and we greatly thank you for that!

no-car.question: ❓I don't have a car — what should I do?
no-car.answer: We do not organize transport or handle transportation. Our job is to organize a great event; yours is to get there. But for your convenience we created the chat
no-car.answer2: . Join, meet people, and find company. Everyone there is great, sociable, and reliable. Want to chat about space topics? Join the thematic

public-transport.question: ❓Can I get to the star party by public transport?
public-transport.answer: Unfortunately, no. The venue is far from public transport routes. You can get there by your own car or join other participants if they are willing to take you. We recommend arranging this in advance via our Telegram channel.

start-time.question: ❓What time does it start?
start-time.answer: Each star party announcement includes a specific start time. This time it is 21:30. Guests are invited to arrive at the site from 20:00 to 21:00. This means we ask you to arrive early — to listen to music, take photos with the Moon and in the sunflowers, watch the sunsets, and breathe in the steppe air. And importantly, to park your car and avoid congestion. We really appreciate that!

price.question: ❓How much does it cost?
price.answer: All our star parties are free. The project grows and develops and we are always grateful for support from our audience and subscribers. For this there are
price.answer2: . Want to support us — we will be grateful!

duration.question: ❓How long does a star party last?
duration.answer: On average the official program from the start of the lecture to telescope observations lasts 2 hours. But for you they will fly by unnoticed, we guarantee. After that you choose — stay to watch the sky with telescopes, or head home. Approximate end time: 00:30.

weather.question: ❓What if it is cloudy or raining?
weather.answer: If the weather forecast is unfavorable (clouds or precipitation), the event may be cancelled or postponed. We always notify participants of such changes via our Telegram channel, so be sure to follow the updates.

telescopes.question: ❓Do I need to bring my own telescope?
telescopes.answer: No, you don't need to bring a telescope. We always have several telescopes available for public use. If you have your own instrument, you can bring it and our experts will help you set it up and use it.

kids.question: ❓Is it suitable for children?
kids.answer: Yes, absolutely! We are very happy when the younger generation takes an interest in science! However, the recommended age is 6 and above. Trust our experience.

photo.question: ❓Can I take photos?
photo.answer: Of course! You can take photographs, but please follow etiquette rules so as not to disturb other participants. Do not use bright flashes or flashlights, as this can ruin nighttime observations.

merch.question: ❓How and where can I buy your merch?
merch.answer: We bring hoodies, t-shirts, car stickers, postcards, and sticker packs to the star party. Want to support the project — buy our merch!

cancel.question: ❓I registered but can't make it. What should I do?
cancel.answer: Simply cancel your booking via the same link. Free slots will appear on the site and people will be able to register.

more-people.question: ❓I registered 1/2/3/4/5 people — can I bring more?
more-people.answer: No, you cannot. Our rules are the same for everyone. Only the number of people you registered will be admitted with your QR code. No exceptions.

late-registration.question: ❓Registration ended and I didn't make it. Can I still come? Please!
late-registration.answer: No, unfortunately. Our rules are the same for everyone. We introduced the registration system for a reason and monitor entry to the star party site.

support.question: ❓I like you! How can I support you?
support.answer2: . We are constantly buying new equipment, upgrading our technical capabilities, creating merch, and various activities. Your support means ❤︎❤︎❤︎

feedback.question: ❓Where can I leave a review about you?
feedback.answer: In all social networks our group is called "Look at the Stars". We value feedback and always read your reviews. Thank you for them!

stargazing-howto.title: How Star Parties Work
stargazing-howto.description: Learn how star parties work in Orenburg: registration, space lectures, telescope observations, and learning to navigate by the night sky. Subscribe to the Look at the Stars Telegram channel and join unique astronomical events under the starry sky!
stargazing-howto.unique_event: Every star party is unique, but we follow a general scenario to make the event interesting and convenient for all participants.
stargazing-howto.event_announcement_text: Star parties in Orenburg are held in favorable weather, so the exact date is only announced 2–3 days before the event. We announce upcoming outings in our Look at the Stars Telegram channel in advance, where we publish announcements and important updates. Subscribing to the channel is essential so you don't miss a new star party.
stargazing-howto.registration_text: Participation requires pre-registration through our website. It is free and takes only one minute. Note: beware of scammers — if someone asks for payment to participate, it is a scam! We welcome donations, but participation is always free.
stargazing-howto.location_text: Star parties take place outside the city, away from urban light pollution, usually 40 km from Orenburg. The exact venue will be revealed after registration. You need to get to the site independently, so plan your route in advance. The last stretch of the route is a dirt road about 300 meters long.
stargazing-howto.arrival_text: Arrive 15–20 minutes before the start to find a comfortable spot on the astronomy field. Bring folding chairs or mats for comfortable seating.
stargazing-howto.lecture_text: As soon as dusk falls, our astro lecture begins. On a large screen we show videos and photos, talking about space in an accessible and interesting way. Lecture topics vary: from 'The Perseids Meteor Shower' to 'What Black Holes Hide'. Lectures are designed for participants aged 8 and above and last about an hour.
stargazing-howto.orientation_text: After the lecture we teach you to find the main stars and constellations, pointing them out in the sky. This part lasts about 20 minutes.
stargazing-howto.telescope_observation_text: Several telescopes are set up at the astronomy site, each aimed at certain space objects. You will be able to look through the telescopes, ask questions, and talk with astronomers.
stargazing-howto.event_duration_text: The entire star party lasts from 2 to 4 hours. You can leave the site at any time, but many stay until the very end, enjoying views of the starry sky.
stargazing-howto.recommendations_text: Please read the Star Party Rules section so that the event is comfortable and safe for everyone.
stargazing-howto.thematic_evenings_text: Sometimes we hold sidewalk astronomy right in the city. Read details about such events in the Where to Look Through a Telescope in Orenburg section.
stargazing-howto.faq: Didn't find an answer to your question? Check the section
stargazing-howto.telegram: or write in our Telegram channel

stargazing-rules.title: Star Party Rules
stargazing-rules.description: Learn the rules of behavior at star parties in Orenburg: what not to do when observing stars and planets through a telescope. Follow the guidelines to make the star party safe and comfortable for all participants.
stargazing-rules.welcome: Welcome to the star parties in Orenburg organized by the Look at the Stars project! To ensure that joint observation of the starry sky and planets is comfortable and safe for all participants, it is important to follow a few simple rules. These guidelines help preserve the atmosphere of nighttime observation and guarantee that everyone can enjoy the view of stars and planets through a telescope.
stargazing-rules.prohibited-title: What Not to Do at Star Parties

prohibited-items-1.title: Use bright light sources
prohibited-items-1.description: Flashlights, phone screens, and any bright lighting devices disrupt the eyes' adaptation to darkness, which reduces visibility of the night sky. Use only red flashlights or special light filters for devices.

prohibited-items-2.title: Light fires and use open flame
prohibited-items-2.description: Star parties take place in fields and open areas where the risk of fire is high. Lighting fires is strictly prohibited.

prohibited-items-3.title: Consume alcohol and smoke
prohibited-items-3.description: Alcoholic beverages, cigarettes, e-cigarettes, vapes, and hookahs are prohibited. This is not only a matter of fire safety but also respect for other participants for whom clean night sky and fresh air are important.

prohibited-items-4.title: Make noise and disturb other participants
prohibited-items-4.description: Loud music, shouting, or other sharp sounds can distract and disturb concentration when observing through a telescope. Respect the silence and the mood of those around you.

recommendations-items-1.title: Take care of nature
recommendations-items-1.description: Take all your litter with you after observations. We try to leave the star party sites as clean as they were before our arrival.

recommendations-items-2.title: Follow safety rules
recommendations-items-2.description: Move carefully to avoid damaging equipment and injuring yourself.

recommendations-items-3.title: Respect other participants
recommendations-items-3.description: Everyone came to the star party to enjoy the beauty of the cosmos. Respectful treatment of one another creates a friendly and comfortable atmosphere.

stargazing-rules.importance-title: Why Is It Important to Follow the Rules?
stargazing-rules.importance-description: Following these guidelines helps make observing the night sky in Orenburg as comfortable as possible for everyone. We create an atmosphere where every participant can witness amazing celestial phenomena, study planets through a telescope, and have an unforgettable experience.
stargazing-rules.join-description: Want to learn more about our events organized in Orenburg?

stargazing-where.title: Where to Look Through a Telescope in Orenburg
stargazing-where.description: Find out where you can look through a telescope in Orenburg. Sidewalk astronomy: free observations of the Moon, planets, and stars on city streets. Want to see more — head out of the city to observe nebulae and galaxies!
stargazing-where.intro: Sidewalk astronomy is a unique format thanks to which the cosmos comes closer. In spring, summer, and autumn we set up telescopes right on the sidewalks and squares of Orenburg so that anyone can come up and take a free look at the stars, planets, and the Moon. It is an excellent opportunity for city residents to get acquainted with astronomy in a relaxed and friendly atmosphere.
stargazing-where.announcement-1: We almost always publish announcements of sidewalk astronomy evenings in our
stargazing-where.announcement-2: , but sometimes our telescopes can be found on city streets without any prior notice. One of the project's inspirers, astro-lecturer Vladimir Ivanovich, often shares his knowledge, showing everyone interested fascinating astronomical objects. Just look for the telescope — and you will definitely end up on an unforgettable tour of the night sky!
stargazing-where.what-you-can-see-title: What can you see through a telescope from the city?
stargazing-where.what-you-can-see-description: Under conditions of urban light pollution, the brightest objects remain visible. For example, you can observe:
stargazing-where.what-you-can-see-1: Craters and mountains on the Moon, especially impressive in the first and last quarter phases;
stargazing-where.what-you-can-see-2: Saturn's rings, which invariably inspire awe;
stargazing-where.what-you-can-see-3: Bright stars and double stars such as Albireo;
stargazing-where.what-you-can-see-4: Planetary parades, if they occur at the right time of year.
stargazing-where.what-you-can-see-conclusion: However, city lighting limits our possibilities. Nebulae, galaxies, meteor showers, and even large star clusters are often hidden from view.
stargazing-where.want-to-see-more: Want to see more? Head outside the city!
stargazing-where.want-to-see-description-1: To observe faint objects such as the Orion Nebula, Andromeda, meteor showers, and star clusters, you need to travel far from city lights. In places like the Orenburg steppe or other remote areas, the night sky astounds with its beauty.
stargazing-where.want-to-see-description-2: We regularly organize field astronomy events where you can enjoy the full palette of space objects. Follow the announcements on our Star Parties page or in our Telegram channel to join one of these observations.

star-map.description: Star map with galaxies, nebulae, comets, supernovae, and other space objects photographed by an amateur telescope
